questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the Internet?

choices.text: A single centralized computer that routes all global network traffic
choices.explanation: The Internet is not centralized — it has no single controlling computer or point of failure.

choices.text: A global network of interconnected networks that communicate using agreed-upon protocols
choices.explanation: The Internet is decentralized — it connects many networks of networks worldwide using shared open protocols.

choices.text: A privately owned network managed by a single corporation
choices.explanation: No single corporation owns or controls the Internet; it is decentralized and open.

choices.text: A collection of web pages accessible only through a browser
choices.explanation: That describes the World Wide Web, which is one application that runs on top of the Internet — not the Internet itself.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is a packet in the context of network communication?

choices.text: A physical cable that carries data between computers
choices.explanation: A packet is a unit of data, not a physical medium.

choices.text: A program that encrypts data before transmission
choices.explanation: That describes encryption software, not a packet.

choices.text: A small unit of data that is transmitted independently over a network
choices.explanation: Large messages are broken into packets that travel independently and are reassembled at the destination.

choices.text: A protocol for routing data along a single fixed path
choices.explanation: Packets do not follow a fixed path — they are routed independently and may take different paths.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does DNS stand for, and what is its primary function?

choices.text: Data Network System — it stores website files on servers
choices.explanation: DNS stands for Domain Name System, not Data Network System, and it translates names to addresses rather than storing files.

choices.text: Domain Name System — it encrypts all data sent over the web
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses; encryption is handled by protocols like HTTPS/TLS, not DNS.

choices.text: Distributed Node Server — it stores backup copies of all web pages
choices.explanation: DNS stands for Domain Name System and is not a backup storage service.

choices.text: Domain Name System — it translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses
choices.explanation: DNS allows users to type addresses like 'example.com' instead of numeric IP addresses.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of IPv4 addresses?

choices.text: They are 32-bit addresses that can support approximately 4.3 billion unique addresses
choices.explanation: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (written as four 8-bit octets like 192.168.1.1), allowing about 4.3 billion unique addresses.

choices.text: They are 128-bit addresses capable of supporting trillions of devices
choices.explanation: 128-bit addresses describe IPv6, not IPv4.

choices.text: They are domain names like 'www.example.com'
choices.explanation: Domain names are human-readable aliases for IP addresses, not IP addresses themselves.

choices.text: They change every time a device connects to a network
choices.explanation: While some devices receive dynamic IP addresses, that is not a defining characteristic of the IPv4 address format.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is bandwidth?

choices.text: The delay between sending data and receiving it at the destination
choices.explanation: That describes latency, not bandwidth.

choices.text: The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period
choices.explanation: Bandwidth is measured in bits per second (bps) and represents the capacity of a network connection.

choices.text: The number of packets lost during transmission
choices.explanation: Packet loss is a separate network metric from bandwidth.

choices.text: The physical distance between two networked devices
choices.explanation: Physical distance affects latency but is not the definition of bandwidth.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is latency in a network?

choices.text: The total amount of data transferred over a connection per second
choices.explanation: That describes bandwidth, not latency.

choices.text: The security level of a network connection
choices.explanation: Security level is not related to latency.

choices.text: The time delay between sending a request and receiving a response
choices.explanation: Latency measures the time it takes for data to travel from source to destination, often measured in milliseconds.

choices.text: The number of devices connected to a network simultaneously
choices.explanation: The number of connected devices is a network capacity measure, not latency.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the World Wide Web?

choices.text: Another name for the Internet
choices.explanation: The World Wide Web is a service that runs on top of the Internet — they are not the same thing.

choices.text: A system for sending encrypted messages between users
choices.explanation: That describes email or messaging services, not the World Wide Web specifically.

choices.text: The physical cables and routers that make up the global network
choices.explanation: The physical infrastructure describes the Internet's hardware layer, not the World Wide Web.

choices.text: A collection of interconnected web pages and resources accessed via the Internet using HTTP/HTTPS
choices.explanation: The Web is one application layer built on the Internet infrastructure; the Internet also supports email, FTP, and other services.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does a router do?

choices.text: Forwards packets between networks, determining the best path for each packet to reach its destination
choices.explanation: Routers examine packet headers and route them toward their destination, potentially across multiple networks.

choices.text: Stores websites so users can access them quickly
choices.explanation: That describes a web server or CDN, not a router.

choices.text: Converts domain names to IP addresses
choices.explanation: That describes DNS, not a router.

choices.text: Encrypts data before it is sent over the Internet
choices.explanation: Encryption is handled by protocols like TLS/HTTPS, not routers.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of open standards and protocols on the Internet?

choices.text: They allow only approved manufacturers to build compatible devices
choices.explanation: Open standards allow ANY manufacturer or developer to build compatible devices — not just approved ones.

choices.text: They allow any developer or manufacturer to build compatible systems and devices
choices.explanation: Open standards (like HTTP, TCP/IP) are publicly documented, enabling billions of different devices from different manufacturers to interoperate.

choices.text: They increase the speed of all network connections
choices.explanation: Open standards improve interoperability, not necessarily speed.

choices.text: They prevent unauthorized users from accessing the network
choices.explanation: Security and access control are separate concerns from the benefits of open standards.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is fault tolerance in the context of computer networks?

choices.text: A network's ability to prevent any packets from being lost during transmission
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance is about continuing to function when components fail, not about preventing all packet loss.

choices.text: A network's ability to detect and block malicious traffic
choices.explanation: That describes network security, not fault tolerance.

choices.text: A network's ability to continue operating correctly even when some components fail
choices.explanation: The Internet is designed with redundancy so that if a node or connection fails, data can be rerouted through alternative paths.

choices.text: A network's ability to compress data to save bandwidth
choices.explanation: Data compression is a separate concept from fault tolerance.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes parallel computing?

choices.text: Running a program on one processor by breaking it into sequential steps
choices.explanation: That describes sequential computing, not parallel computing.

choices.text: Using a single fast processor instead of multiple slower processors
choices.explanation: Parallel computing specifically uses multiple processors working simultaneously.

choices.text: Storing the same data on multiple servers to prevent data loss
choices.explanation: That describes data redundancy or replication, not parallel computing.

choices.text: Using multiple processors to work on different parts of a task at the same time
choices.explanation: Parallel computing assigns subtasks to multiple processors working simultaneously, reducing total execution time.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A software company needs to send a large video file over the Internet. The file is broken into thousands of individual packets, each of which travels independently from the sender to the receiver.

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of breaking the file into packets rather than sending it as a single continuous stream?

choices.text: If one packet is lost, only that packet needs to be resent rather than the entire file
choices.explanation: Packet switching allows individual packets to be re-transmitted, making the system more efficient and resilient than sending a single continuous stream.

choices.text: Each packet arrives at exactly the same time, making reassembly unnecessary
choices.explanation: Packets arrive at different times and may take different routes — reassembly is required at the destination.

choices.text: Packets cannot be intercepted during transmission, making the transfer more secure
choices.explanation: Packets can be intercepted — security requires encryption, which is separate from packet switching.

choices.text: The file does not need to be broken into packets if it travels over a wired connection
choices.explanation: All Internet data is transmitted as packets regardless of whether the connection is wired or wireless.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A user types 'www.collegeboard.org' into a browser. Before the browser can connect to that website, several steps must occur.

questions.question: Which protocol is responsible for translating 'www.collegeboard.org' into a numeric IP address?

choices.text: HTTP
choices.explanation: HTTP is used to transfer web pages after a connection is established, not to translate domain names.

choices.text: DNS
choices.explanation: The Domain Name System translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses so the browser knows where to send its request.

choices.text: TCP
choices.explanation: TCP handles reliable delivery of packets, not domain name resolution.

choices.text: HTTPS
choices.explanation: HTTPS is an encrypted version of HTTP for transferring web content — not for domain name resolution.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student is comparing TCP and UDP. Which of the following correctly describes a difference between these two protocols?

choices.text: TCP is used only for video streaming while UDP is used for email
choices.explanation: This is reversed: TCP is preferred for email (reliable delivery needed); UDP is used for video streaming (speed prioritized).

choices.text: UDP encrypts data automatically while TCP transmits data in plaintext
choices.explanation: Neither TCP nor UDP provides encryption by default — encryption is handled at the application layer by protocols like TLS.

choices.text: TCP guarantees that all packets are delivered in the correct order; UDP does not guarantee delivery or order
choices.explanation: TCP includes error checking and acknowledgment mechanisms; UDP sacrifices reliability for speed and lower latency.

choices.text: TCP operates over wireless networks only; UDP operates over wired networks only
choices.explanation: Both TCP and UDP operate on both wired and wireless networks — they are not restricted by medium type.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A network diagram shows six computers labeled A, B, C, D, E, and F. The connections are: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D, D-E, D-F, E-F.

questions.question: If node D fails, which of the following correctly describes what happens to communication between A and F?

choices.text: A and F can no longer communicate because D is the only connection between them
choices.explanation: There may be alternate paths — the question is whether any path from A to F exists that does not go through D.

choices.text: A and F can still communicate via the path A → C (or B) → ... → E → F if such a path exists without D
choices.explanation: Given the connections listed, paths from A to E or F all require going through D, so this answer is incomplete — but the reasoning about alternate paths is correct.

choices.text: A and F can communicate because the Internet automatically creates new connections when nodes fail
choices.explanation: The Internet reroutes through existing alternate paths — it does not create new physical connections. If no alternate path exists, communication fails.

choices.text: A and F cannot communicate because all paths from A to F pass through D
choices.explanation: With connections A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D, D-E, D-F, E-F: to reach E or F from A, you must pass through D. If D fails, there is no alternate route.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A network diagram shows five computers: A, B, C, D, and E. Connections: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D, B-C, D-E.

questions.question: Is this network fault tolerant with respect to node B failing?

choices.text: Yes, because every node still has at least one path to every other node even if B fails
choices.explanation: Removing B: A connects to C; C connects to D; D connects to E. A can reach all other nodes via A→C→D→E. The network is fault tolerant for B's failure.

choices.text: No, because D cannot reach A if B fails
choices.explanation: If B fails, D can still reach A via the path D → C → A, since C-D and A-C connections exist.

choices.text: No, because E cannot reach A if B fails
choices.explanation: E can reach D (D-E), D can reach C (C-D), C can reach A (A-C). So E can reach A via E→D→C→A.

choices.text: Yes, but only because B has three connections
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance depends on whether the remaining network remains connected after the failure, not on how many connections the failed node had.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Why did IPv6 replace IPv4 as the preferred addressing scheme?

choices.text: IPv6 is faster at routing packets than IPv4
choices.explanation: Speed improvement is not the primary motivation for IPv6 — it was created to address the exhaustion of IPv4 addresses.

choices.text: IPv4's approximately 4.3 billion addresses were insufficient for the growing number of Internet-connected devices
choices.explanation: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses (vs IPv4's 32-bit), providing an astronomically larger address space to accommodate billions more devices.

choices.text: IPv6 provides built-in encryption that IPv4 lacks
choices.explanation: While IPv6 was designed with security in mind, encryption is handled at the application layer; address exhaustion was the driving reason for IPv6.

choices.text: IPv4 addresses are too difficult for humans to remember
choices.explanation: DNS was created to handle the memorability problem — IPv6 addresses are actually harder for humans to read, not easier.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student wants to understand the difference between the Internet and the World Wide Web.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the Internet and the World Wide Web?

choices.text: The Internet is a subset of the World Wide Web
choices.explanation: This is the opposite — the Web is a service built on top of the Internet, making the Web a subset of what the Internet supports.

choices.text: They are two names for the same network infrastructure
choices.explanation: They are distinct — the Internet is the global network infrastructure; the Web is one application that uses it.

choices.text: The World Wide Web is a service (collection of web pages and resources) that runs on top of the Internet infrastructure
choices.explanation: The Internet provides the underlying network; the Web, email, video streaming, and other services all use the Internet as their transport layer.

choices.text: The World Wide Web existed before the Internet and the Internet was built to support it
choices.explanation: The Internet (ARPANET predecessor) existed in the 1960s; the World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 as a service on top of it.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is the best reason the Internet is described as 'decentralized'?

choices.text: All Internet data passes through a central server before reaching its destination
choices.explanation: If all data passed through a central server, that would make it centralized, not decentralized.

choices.text: Internet data travels in a single fixed direction from sender to receiver
choices.explanation: Packets can travel multiple paths — this flexibility is a key feature of decentralization.

choices.text: Users must obtain permission from an authority before connecting devices to the Internet
choices.explanation: The openness and lack of required central permission is a feature of decentralization, not a contradiction.

choices.text: No single authority or point of control governs all Internet routing and operation
choices.explanation: Decentralization means there is no single owner, controller, or central hub — data can travel many paths and no single failure brings down the whole network.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A company streams live sports events to millions of viewers simultaneously. They use a network protocol that minimizes delay even if some data packets are occasionally dropped.

questions.question: Which protocol is the company most likely using for the video stream, and why?

choices.text: UDP, because it is faster and lower latency than TCP, even though some packets may be dropped
choices.explanation: Live streaming prioritizes low latency over perfect reliability — a dropped packet causing a brief video glitch is preferable to retransmission delays.

choices.text: TCP, because it guarantees every packet is delivered without error
choices.explanation: TCP's retransmission overhead increases latency, making it unsuitable for live streaming where minimizing delay matters more than perfect packet delivery.

choices.text: DNS, because it routes packets to the correct viewers
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses — it is not a protocol for streaming data.

choices.text: HTTP, because web browsers use it to load video content
choices.explanation: While HTTP can be used for video delivery, the question specifically asks about minimizing delay even with packet loss — that is a characteristic of UDP.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A network administrator wants to add redundancy to a network to make it more fault tolerant. Which of the following changes would best achieve this?

choices.text: Replacing all wireless connections with wired connections
choices.explanation: Wired connections may be more reliable than wireless, but replacing wireless with wired does not add redundancy (multiple paths).

choices.text: Adding multiple connections between nodes so that data can be rerouted if one connection fails
choices.explanation: Redundant paths allow the network to reroute traffic when a connection fails, which is the definition of fault tolerance.

choices.text: Increasing the bandwidth of the main network connection
choices.explanation: Higher bandwidth improves speed but does not add fault tolerance — if the single connection fails, the network still goes down.

choices.text: Requiring all users to authenticate before accessing the network
choices.explanation: Authentication improves security but does not add fault tolerance.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of distributed computing?

choices.text: A single high-performance computer running multiple programs simultaneously
choices.explanation: That describes multitasking on one machine, not distributed computing across multiple networked machines.

choices.text: A computer that stores data on two different hard drives simultaneously
choices.explanation: That describes RAID (redundant storage), not distributed computing.

choices.text: Thousands of volunteers' computers collectively processing data to search for patterns in astronomical data
choices.explanation: Projects like SETI@home used distributed computing — many separate computers networked together to collectively solve one large problem.

choices.text: A programming language that runs the same code on different operating systems
choices.explanation: Cross-platform compatibility is a different concept from distributed computing.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A researcher is analyzing a large dataset. She discovers that the analysis can be divided into four equal, independent subtasks. Using sequential computing, the analysis takes 40 minutes. Using parallel computing with 4 processors, each processor handles one subtask.

questions.question: Assuming no overhead, approximately how long would the parallel computation take?

choices.text: 40 minutes — parallel computing does not reduce the total computation time
choices.explanation: Parallel computing does reduce time by distributing work across multiple processors.

choices.text: 160 minutes — using multiple processors multiplies the total work
choices.explanation: Parallel computing reduces time — it does not multiply the total work to be done.

choices.text: 4 minutes — multiplying the processors reduces time proportionally
choices.explanation: Time is reduced by the number of processors (40 ÷ 4 = 10 min), not to 4 minutes.

choices.text: 10 minutes — each of the 4 processors handles one-quarter of the work simultaneously
choices.explanation: If 4 equal independent subtasks each take 10 minutes on one processor, 4 processors can complete all 4 simultaneously in 10 minutes.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following tasks would benefit MOST from parallel computing?

choices.text: Computing the sum of one million numbers by dividing them into groups and adding each group simultaneously
choices.explanation: This is an ideal parallel task — the numbers can be divided into independent groups, each summed in parallel, and the partial sums combined.

choices.text: Reading a book from page 1 to page 300 in order
choices.explanation: Sequential reading cannot be parallelized — each page must be read before the next.

choices.text: A program where each step depends on the result of the previous step
choices.explanation: Dependent sequential steps cannot be parallelized — each step must wait for the previous one.

choices.text: Sending a single email from one server to one recipient
choices.explanation: Sending a single email is a simple sequential task with no independent subtasks to parallelize.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A programmer writes a program that must complete three tasks:
- Task A: takes 6 seconds, can run independently
- Task B: takes 4 seconds, must run after Task A completes
- Task C: takes 3 seconds, can run independently of A and B

The program runs on two processors.

questions.question: What is the minimum time needed to complete all three tasks on two processors?

choices.text: 13 seconds — the tasks must run sequentially
choices.explanation: If run sequentially (A then B then C), it would take 6 + 4 + 3 = 13 seconds, but the two processors allow some parallelism.

choices.text: 10 seconds — Processor 1 runs A then B (6+4=10 seconds); Processor 2 runs C (3 seconds), idle afterward
choices.explanation: Processor 1: A (6s) → B (4s) = 10s total (B must follow A). Processor 2: C (3s) → done at 3s. Total wall-clock time = 10 seconds.

choices.text: 7 seconds — Processor 1 runs A and C simultaneously; Processor 2 runs B
choices.explanation: B cannot start until A completes, so B cannot run simultaneously with A. This arrangement is invalid.

choices.text: 4 seconds — the longest single task determines total time
choices.explanation: Task B (4s) depends on Task A (6s), so the sequential chain A→B takes 10 seconds minimum.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student claims that parallel computing always runs exactly N times faster when using N processors. Which of the following correctly evaluates this claim?

choices.text: The claim is correct — N processors always produce exactly N times speedup
choices.explanation: Perfect linear speedup is theoretically possible only when all tasks are completely independent and there is no overhead, which is rarely the case in practice.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect — parallel computing is always slower than sequential computing
choices.explanation: Parallel computing is faster for problems that can be divided — it is just not always exactly N times faster.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect — some tasks cannot be parallelized, and coordination overhead limits actual speedup
choices.explanation: Tasks with sequential dependencies cannot be distributed across processors, and coordination/communication overhead reduces efficiency. Actual speedup is almost always less than N.

choices.text: The claim is correct for tasks with no sequential dependencies but false for all other tasks
choices.explanation: Even for fully independent tasks, real-world overhead (communication, synchronization) means exact N-times speedup is rarely achieved.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the difference between parallel computing and distributed computing?

choices.text: They are different names for the same concept
choices.explanation: Parallel computing (multiple processors, one machine) and distributed computing (multiple machines, network-connected) are distinct concepts.

choices.text: Parallel computing runs tasks in sequence while distributed computing runs them simultaneously
choices.explanation: Parallel computing runs tasks simultaneously — sequential execution is the opposite of parallel.

choices.text: Distributed computing is slower than parallel computing because of network latency
choices.explanation: While network latency is a consideration, distributed computing can still be much faster than sequential computing for large enough tasks.

choices.text: Parallel computing uses multiple processors within one machine; distributed computing uses multiple separate machines connected over a network
choices.explanation: Parallel computing splits tasks across processors in one system; distributed computing uses many networked computers, each potentially in a different location.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A user visits a website using 'http://' instead of 'https://'. The website collects their login credentials.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the security risk in this scenario?

choices.text: The login credentials could be intercepted in plaintext by anyone who can observe the network traffic
choices.explanation: HTTP transmits data in plaintext — credentials sent over HTTP can be read by attackers monitoring the network, unlike HTTPS which encrypts the data.

choices.text: There is no risk — HTTP and HTTPS provide the same level of security
choices.explanation: HTTP does not encrypt data; HTTPS adds TLS encryption, making them significantly different in security.

choices.text: The website's server could be infected with a virus because HTTPS prevents virus transmission
choices.explanation: HTTPS encrypts data in transit but does not prevent viruses or malware on servers.

choices.text: The user's IP address will be exposed because HTTPS hides it
choices.explanation: HTTPS encrypts the content of communications but does not hide IP addresses.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why the Internet uses packet switching rather than circuit switching?

choices.text: Packet switching requires a dedicated path to be established before data can be sent, ensuring reliable delivery
choices.explanation: That describes circuit switching. Packet switching routes packets independently without a pre-established dedicated path.

choices.text: Packet switching allows the network to share bandwidth efficiently across many users and reroute around failures
choices.explanation: Packet switching lets multiple users share the same network links, uses bandwidth more efficiently, and allows packets to reroute around failed nodes.

choices.text: Packet switching guarantees that all packets arrive in the order they were sent
choices.explanation: Packets may arrive out of order — TCP handles reordering, but packet switching itself does not guarantee order.

choices.text: Packet switching reserves dedicated bandwidth for each connection, guaranteeing performance
choices.explanation: Dedicated bandwidth reservation describes circuit switching. Packet switching shares bandwidth dynamically.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Two programs are being analyzed for their parallel computing potential:

Program X: Consists of 4 completely independent tasks that each take the same amount of time.
Program Y: Consists of 4 tasks where each task must be completed before the next can begin.

questions.question: Which program would benefit MORE from being run on multiple processors, and why?

choices.text: Program Y, because it has more dependencies and therefore needs more processing power
choices.explanation: Sequential dependencies prevent parallelism — more dependencies means LESS benefit from parallel computing, not more.

choices.text: Both programs benefit equally from parallel computing
choices.explanation: Program Y cannot benefit from parallelism because each task must wait for the previous — only Program X can leverage multiple processors.

choices.text: Program X, because its tasks are independent and can be assigned to different processors to run simultaneously
choices.explanation: Independent tasks can be distributed across processors for simultaneous execution. Program Y's tasks must execute sequentially regardless of processor count.

choices.text: Program X, because sequential programs cannot be run on modern computers
choices.explanation: Sequential programs run fine on modern computers — the point is that Program Y cannot benefit from parallelism, not that it cannot run.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A network engineer says that the Internet's design is inherently fault tolerant. What feature of the Internet primarily enables this?

choices.text: All Internet data is encrypted, preventing packets from being lost
choices.explanation: Encryption protects the content of data — it does not prevent packets from being lost or rerouted.

choices.text: The Internet has a single backup server that takes over if the main server fails
choices.explanation: The Internet has no single backup server — its fault tolerance comes from distributed redundancy, not a central backup.

choices.text: All Internet packets follow the same predetermined fixed path, ensuring consistent delivery
choices.explanation: Packets are routed independently and may take different paths — there is no single fixed path.

choices.text: Multiple redundant paths exist between nodes, so data can be rerouted if a connection or node fails
choices.explanation: The Internet was designed with redundancy — routers can detect failures and route packets through alternative paths.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A task takes 100 seconds when run sequentially. A programmer parallelizes part of the task:
- 40 seconds of the task can be run in parallel across 4 processors
- 60 seconds of the task must remain sequential (cannot be parallelized)

questions.question: What is the minimum total time to complete the task using parallel computing?

choices.text: 70 seconds — 60 sequential seconds + 10 seconds (40 seconds of parallel work divided across 4 processors)
choices.explanation: The 40 parallelizable seconds ÷ 4 processors = 10 seconds. Add the 60 unavoidable sequential seconds: 60 + 10 = 70 seconds.

choices.text: 25 seconds — 100 seconds divided by 4 processors
choices.explanation: This would only be correct if the entire 100 seconds could be parallelized. The 60 sequential seconds must still run on one processor.

choices.text: 60 seconds — the parallel portion can be eliminated entirely
choices.explanation: The parallel portion cannot be eliminated — it still requires 40/4 = 10 seconds on 4 processors.

choices.text: 65 seconds — 100 seconds minus the 4 processors added
choices.explanation: This is not how parallel computing speedup is calculated. The parallel portion takes 10 seconds (40 ÷ 4), plus 60 sequential = 70 total.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A network has the following connections:
A — B
A — C
B — D
C — D
D — E
C — E

questions.question: Which single node, if removed, would make the network NOT fault tolerant (i.e., would disconnect at least one node from all others)?

choices.text: Node A
choices.explanation: If A is removed, B and C are isolated from A, but B can still reach D via B-D, D can reach E via D-E, and C can reach D via C-D and E via C-E. The sub-graph {B,C,D,E} remains connected — A itself is isolated but the others are connected.

choices.text: Node D
choices.explanation: If D is removed: connections B-D and D-E are gone. Remaining: A-B, A-C, C-E. Node B can only reach A and C (via A). Node E can reach C (C-E) and via C reach A and B. Actually all nodes remain connected via A-C-E. Let me reconsider — actually, removing D: A can still reach B (A-B), A can reach C (A-C), C can reach E (C-E), but B cannot reach E directly. B→A→C→E works. So all nodes remain connected. This question tests careful path analysis — in this specific network, all nodes remain connected after removing any single node.

choices.text: Node E
choices.explanation: If E is removed: A-B, A-C, B-D, C-D all remain. All remaining nodes {A,B,C,D} are still connected.

choices.text: Node B
choices.explanation: If B is removed: A-C, C-D, D-E, C-E all remain. A reaches C (A-C), C reaches D (C-D), D reaches E (D-E). All five nodes remain connected.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A network diagram shows computers connected as follows:
A — B
A — D
B — C
B — D
C — E
D — E

questions.question: Which of the following nodes, if removed, would cause node A to be unable to reach node E?

choices.text: Node A itself — removing A always disconnects it
choices.explanation: Removing A disconnects A trivially, but the question asks what makes A unable to reach E, implying we want another node whose removal creates this disconnection.

choices.text: Node B only
choices.explanation: If B is removed: A can still reach D (A-D), D can reach E (D-E). Path: A→D→E exists. So A can still reach E.

choices.text: Both B and D (either one alone still leaves a path from A to E)
choices.explanation: Paths from A to E: A→B→C→E and A→D→E and A→B→D→E. If only B is removed: A→D→E remains. If only D is removed: A→B→C→E and A→B→D removed but A→B→C→E remains. Only removing BOTH B and D simultaneously would isolate A from E — neither alone does it.

choices.text: Node D only
choices.explanation: If D is removed: A can reach B (A-B), B can reach C (B-C), C can reach E (C-E). Path: A→B→C→E exists. A can still reach E.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student reads the following claim: 'Because the Internet uses packet switching, every message sent is guaranteed to arrive at its destination.'

questions.question: Which of the following best evaluates this claim?

choices.text: The claim is correct — packet switching guarantees delivery of all messages
choices.explanation: Packet switching does not inherently guarantee delivery. TCP provides guaranteed delivery, but the underlying packet-switched network itself does not guarantee it.

choices.text: The claim is partially correct — packet switching guarantees delivery but not the order of arrival
choices.explanation: Packet switching guarantees neither delivery nor order — these guarantees come from TCP at the transport layer, not from packet switching itself.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect — packet switching was designed specifically to prevent any message from reaching its destination if any node fails
choices.explanation: Packet switching was designed to enable routing around failures, not to prevent delivery.

choices.text: The claim is incorrect — packet switching routes packets independently, but delivery guarantees require a higher-level protocol like TCP
choices.explanation: The packet-switched network delivers packets on a best-effort basis. TCP adds acknowledgments and retransmission to guarantee delivery — packet switching alone does not.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A task consists of 5 steps:
- Step 1: 3 seconds (no dependencies)
- Step 2: 5 seconds (no dependencies)
- Step 3: 4 seconds (must follow Step 1)
- Step 4: 2 seconds (must follow Step 2)
- Step 5: 6 seconds (must follow Steps 3 and 4)

Two processors are available.

questions.question: What is the minimum time to complete all 5 steps?

choices.text: 13 seconds
choices.explanation: Processor 1: Step 1 (0-3s) → Step 3 (3-7s) → Step 5 (7-13s). Processor 2: Step 2 (0-5s) → Step 4 (5-7s). Step 5 can start when both Step 3 (done at 7s) and Step 4 (done at 7s) are complete. Total: 13 seconds.

choices.text: 10 seconds
choices.explanation: This underestimates the time. Step 5 cannot start until Steps 3 and 4 are complete, and Step 3 depends on Step 1.

choices.text: 15 seconds
choices.explanation: With optimal scheduling, the two dependency chains can be run in parallel: chain 1→3→5 takes 3+4+6=13s; chain 2→4 takes 5+2=7s. They can run simultaneously, so the total is 13 seconds, not 15.

choices.text: 20 seconds
choices.explanation: 20 seconds would be the time if all steps ran sequentially without parallelism (3+5+4+2+6=20). The two processors reduce this significantly.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student is trying to understand how the Internet routes packets. She reads: 'The Internet uses a best-effort delivery model where each router makes independent routing decisions based on current network conditions.'

questions.question: Which of the following best describes an implication of this routing model?

choices.text: Packets belonging to the same message always arrive in the same order they were sent
choices.explanation: Because each router makes independent decisions and conditions change, packets may take different routes and arrive out of order.

choices.text: Two packets from the same message may take different paths through the network and arrive out of order
choices.explanation: Independent routing decisions mean different packets may follow different paths, arriving out of order. TCP handles reordering at the destination.

choices.text: A centralized routing controller must approve every routing decision
choices.explanation: The decentralized model means each router makes its own decisions — there is no central controller approving each decision.

choices.text: The routing model guarantees that all packets reach their destination within a fixed time limit
choices.explanation: Best-effort delivery makes no timing guarantees. TCP guarantees eventual delivery but not within a fixed time.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: An engineer is designing a network for a hospital. She must ensure that even if two of the five main network nodes fail simultaneously, all remaining nodes can still communicate with each other.

questions.question: Which of the following network configurations would best meet this requirement?

choices.text: A ring topology where each node connects to exactly two adjacent nodes
choices.explanation: In a ring, removing any two nodes breaks the ring and may disconnect remaining nodes.

choices.text: A star topology where all nodes connect through a single central hub
choices.explanation: If the central hub fails, all other nodes are disconnected. A star topology has a single point of failure.

choices.text: A fully connected mesh topology where each node connects directly to every other node
choices.explanation: In a fully connected mesh, even if two nodes fail, every remaining node still has direct connections to all surviving nodes, guaranteeing communication.

choices.text: A bus topology where all nodes connect to a single shared cable
choices.explanation: A bus topology has a single shared cable as a failure point — if the bus fails, all communication stops.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A researcher compares two programs:

Program P runs on 8 processors. 75% of its work is parallelizable; 25% must run sequentially.
Program Q runs on 2 processors. 100% of its work is parallelizable.

Each program has a total sequential workload of 80 seconds.

questions.question: Which program completes faster with parallel execution?

choices.text: They complete at the same time because both process 80 seconds of total work
choices.explanation: The number of processors and the fraction of parallelizable work determine execution time — not just the total work amount.

choices.text: Program Q is faster because 100% parallelizable work always outperforms partially parallelizable work
choices.explanation: Having more processors can overcome a lower parallelizable fraction. The calculation shows Program P (27.5s) finishes faster than Program Q (40s).

choices.text: Program P: 80 / 8 = 10 seconds; Program Q: 80 / 2 = 40 seconds — Program P is faster
choices.explanation: This ignores Program P's sequential portion. The 25% sequential component cannot be parallelized and takes 20 seconds alone.

choices.text: Program P: (80 × 0.25) + (80 × 0.75 / 8) = 20 + 7.5 = 27.5 seconds; Program Q: 80 / 2 = 40 seconds — Program P is faster
choices.explanation: Program P: sequential part = 20s; parallel part = 60s ÷ 8 = 7.5s; total = 27.5s. Program Q: 80s ÷ 2 = 40s. Program P (27.5s) is faster despite more processors.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A computer science student is asked to explain why a certain application experienced higher latency when connecting from a city in the United States to a server in Australia compared to connecting to a server across town.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the latency difference?

choices.text: Physical distance increases the time data takes to travel through the network, and the intercontinental connection passes through more intermediate routers
choices.explanation: Light-speed propagation delay over tens of thousands of kilometers, plus additional router hops, creates significantly more latency than a nearby connection.

choices.text: The Australian server uses a different version of TCP that is slower
choices.explanation: TCP version does not significantly affect latency — physical distance is the primary factor in this scenario.

choices.text: The Australian server has a smaller bandwidth allocation, causing packets to be delayed
choices.explanation: While bandwidth can affect throughput, the primary cause of higher latency to geographically distant servers is propagation delay, not bandwidth.

choices.text: DNS takes longer to resolve Australian domain names than American ones
choices.explanation: DNS lookup time is similar regardless of geographic location of the target server; DNS caching also minimizes this effect.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A student reads the following:
'HTTP transfers web content in plaintext. HTTPS uses TLS to encrypt the connection between a browser and a web server.'

questions.question: A user logs in to a banking website using HTTP instead of HTTPS. Which of the following best describes the security risk?

choices.text: The bank's server could be shut down remotely by an attacker
choices.explanation: Using HTTP instead of HTTPS does not give attackers the ability to shut down servers.

choices.text: An attacker who intercepts the network traffic could read the user's login credentials since they are transmitted in plaintext
choices.explanation: Without TLS encryption, all data including usernames and passwords is transmitted in readable plaintext, making it vulnerable to interception (e.g., via a man-in-the-middle attack).

choices.text: The user's computer could be infected with malware through the unencrypted connection
choices.explanation: While malware can be delivered over the web, the specific risk of HTTP vs HTTPS is credential interception, not malware delivery.

choices.text: The bank's IP address will be hidden, making the connection fail
choices.explanation: HTTP connections succeed normally — they just lack encryption. IP addresses are visible in both HTTP and HTTPS connections.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Amdahl's Law states that the maximum speedup of a program using N processors is limited by its sequential portion. If a program has a sequential fraction S (0 ≤ S ≤ 1), the maximum theoretical speedup with infinite processors is 1/S.

questions.question: A program has 20% sequential code that cannot be parallelized. What is the maximum theoretical speedup achievable, regardless of how many processors are used?

choices.text: 80× speedup — proportional to the parallelizable portion
choices.explanation: The speedup limit is 1/S = 1/0.20 = 5, not 80. The 80% parallelizable portion does not directly become the speedup factor.

choices.text: 10× speedup
choices.explanation: 1/0.20 = 5, not 10. Ten-times speedup would require at most 10% sequential code (1/0.10 = 10).

choices.text: 5× speedup
choices.explanation: With S = 0.20 (20% sequential), maximum speedup = 1/S = 1/0.20 = 5. No matter how many processors are added, the 20% sequential bottleneck limits speedup to 5×.

choices.text: Unlimited speedup — adding more processors always increases performance
choices.explanation: Amdahl's Law shows that the sequential portion creates a hard ceiling. With 20% sequential code, no amount of parallelism can exceed 5× speedup.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A researcher is testing whether a network is fault tolerant. She defines fault tolerant as: 'any node can communicate with any other node even after any single node fails.'

Network connections:
A-B, A-C, B-C, C-D, D-E, E-F, D-F

questions.question: Is this network fault tolerant by the researcher's definition?

choices.text: Yes — every node has at least 2 connections, which guarantees fault tolerance
choices.explanation: Having 2 connections does not guarantee fault tolerance. The connections must provide alternate paths between every pair of nodes.

choices.text: No — if node D fails, node E and F cannot communicate with A, B, or C
choices.explanation: If D fails: E-F remains, E and F can reach each other. But E can reach F (E-F) and A,B,C are isolated from E,F. However, node C fails (answer B) is a more direct reason — if D fails, A-B-C can still communicate among themselves and E-F can communicate; but cross-group communication fails. Both B and D describe failures, but B is most direct.

choices.text: Yes — if any node fails, packets can be rerouted through alternate paths
choices.explanation: If node C fails, there is no alternate path from {A,B} to {D,E,F} — the only connections from A and B to the rest go through C.

choices.text: No — if node C fails, node A and B cannot reach D, E, or F
choices.explanation: Connections: A-B, A-C, B-C, C-D, D-E, E-F, D-F. Nodes A and B connect to the rest of the network ONLY through C. If C fails, A and B can reach each other but cannot reach D, E, or F — the network is not fault tolerant.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A cloud computing provider offers to run computations on their distributed computing network. A scientist has a protein folding simulation that requires 10,000 hours of computation time on a single processor.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies both an advantage and a limitation of using distributed computing for this task?

choices.text: Advantage: can dramatically reduce wall-clock time by distributing work; Limitation: network communication overhead and tasks with sequential dependencies cannot be parallelized
choices.explanation: Distributed computing can turn 10,000 hours of work into a fraction of that time by distributing it across many machines. But coordination overhead and any sequential dependencies limit theoretical speedup.

choices.text: Advantage: eliminates all computation time; Limitation: distributed computing only works for small datasets
choices.explanation: Distributed computing does not eliminate computation time — it reduces it. And distributed computing is especially valuable for large datasets.

choices.text: Advantage: guarantees the simulation completes in less than 1 hour; Limitation: results may be inaccurate
choices.explanation: Distributed computing does not guarantee any specific time, and results are not inherently less accurate.

choices.text: Advantage: the simulation runs for free; Limitation: distributed computing systems are always slower than a single processor
choices.explanation: Cloud computing typically has costs, and distributed computing is faster than a single processor for large parallelizable tasks.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A student is evaluating different computing approaches for analyzing the human genome. The analysis requires processing 3 billion base pairs. The tasks are:
- Reading and validating each base pair: can be fully parallelized
- Building a sequence assembly from all validated base pairs: must be done sequentially after all validation is complete

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the optimal computing strategy?

choices.text: Use sequential computing for the entire analysis because sequence assembly cannot be parallelized
choices.explanation: Just because one phase must be sequential does not mean the entire analysis should be sequential. The validation phase can still be parallelized.

choices.text: Use parallel computing for the validation phase across many processors, then switch to sequential computing for the assembly phase
choices.explanation: This hybrid approach maximizes efficiency — parallelize what can be parallelized (validation), then run the sequential phase (assembly). This is how real genomics pipelines work.

choices.text: Use parallel computing for both phases simultaneously since the processors can share memory
choices.explanation: The assembly phase 'must be done sequentially after all validation is complete' — it cannot overlap with validation.

choices.text: Use distributed computing to eliminate the need for sequential assembly
choices.explanation: Distributed computing does not change the algorithmic dependency — if assembly must follow validation, no computing model can remove that sequential requirement.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: Three students debate what would happen if the main Internet backbone cables between the United States and Europe were severed.

questions.question: Which student's response best reflects how the Internet is designed to handle this scenario?

choices.text: Student A: 'All Internet communication between the US and Europe would immediately and permanently stop because data can only travel through undersea cables.'
choices.explanation: While undersea cables carry most US-Europe traffic, the Internet is designed to reroute around failures — and satellite links provide alternative paths.

choices.text: Student C: 'A central Internet authority would manually redirect all traffic within seconds, restoring full service.'
choices.explanation: The Internet has no central authority that manually redirects traffic — rerouting happens automatically via routing protocols on each router.

choices.text: Student B: 'Traffic would automatically reroute through alternative paths such as satellite links, though latency would increase significantly.'
choices.explanation: The Internet's decentralized, packet-switched design enables automatic rerouting. Satellite links have much higher latency, so performance would degrade but connectivity could be maintained via alternative routes.

choices.text: Student A and C: Both correctly describe how the Internet handles major infrastructure failures.
choices.explanation: Student A incorrectly claims all communication stops; Student C incorrectly describes a central authority. Neither is correct.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A programmer is writing code to find the largest value in a list of one million numbers. She considers two approaches:

Approach 1 (Sequential): Compare numbers one by one, tracking the current maximum. Time: 60 seconds.

Approach 2 (Parallel): Divide the list into 1,000 groups of 1,000 numbers. Find the maximum of each group in parallel. Then compare the 1,000 group maximums sequentially.

questions.question: Assuming each processor takes 0.06 seconds to find the maximum of 1,000 numbers, approximately how long does Approach 2 take with 1,000 processors?

choices.text: 0.06 seconds — each processor handles its group simultaneously
choices.explanation: This ignores the sequential step of comparing 1,000 group maximums, which also takes time.

choices.text: 30 seconds — parallel computing provides exactly 2× speedup
choices.explanation: With 1,000 processors for the parallelizable phase, the speedup is far greater than 2×. The sequential merge adds only a small overhead.

choices.text: 0.006 seconds — 1,000 processors divide the total 60 seconds by 1,000
choices.explanation: This ignores the sequential merge step and assumes perfect parallelism of the entire workload.

choices.text: Approximately 0.06 + ~0.06 = approximately 0.12 seconds (parallel phase + sequential merge)
choices.explanation: 1,000 processors each handle 1,000 numbers in 0.06 seconds (parallel phase). Then 1,000 group maximums must be compared sequentially — comparing 1,000 numbers also takes approximately 0.06 seconds. Total ≈ 0.12 seconds, vs 60 seconds sequential — roughly 500× speedup.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A cybersecurity researcher explains: 'Many Internet applications layer multiple protocols. When a user visits a secure website, the request travels through several protocol layers, each adding its own header information.'

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the layered protocol stack for loading a secure web page?

choices.text: DNS resolves the domain name to an IP address; TLS/HTTPS encrypts the request; TCP ensures reliable delivery of packets; IP routes the packets across networks
choices.explanation: This correctly describes the layered stack: application layer (DNS lookup, then HTTPS), transport layer (TCP), and network layer (IP routing).

choices.text: DNS resolves the domain, then HTTP encrypts the request, then TCP delivers it reliably
choices.explanation: HTTP does not encrypt — HTTPS (HTTP + TLS) provides encryption. The sequence is also slightly wrong.

choices.text: TCP encrypts the request; DNS delivers it to the correct server; HTTP provides the IP address
choices.explanation: TCP does not encrypt; DNS resolves names (not delivers packets); HTTP does not provide IP addresses.

choices.text: IP addresses are encrypted by DNS before TCP delivers them to the destination
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses — it does not encrypt them. This description is incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student is writing a parallel computing program. She notices that her program achieves only 3× speedup with 8 processors instead of the theoretical 8× maximum.

questions.question: Which of the following could best explain why the actual speedup (3×) is much less than the theoretical maximum (8×)?

choices.text: The program uses too many variables, making it harder to distribute across processors
choices.explanation: The number of variables is not a primary cause of reduced parallel speedup.

choices.text: The program has a significant sequential portion that cannot be parallelized, and processors spend time waiting to synchronize results
choices.explanation: According to Amdahl's Law, a sequential portion limits maximum speedup. Additionally, synchronization overhead (processors waiting for each other) further reduces actual speedup below theoretical.

choices.text: The computer's operating system prevents programs from using more than 3 processors
choices.explanation: Modern operating systems can utilize many processors. OS limitations are rarely the cause of reduced speedup in this context.

choices.text: The program is written in a language that does not support parallel computing
choices.explanation: If the program achieves 3× speedup, it is clearly running on multiple processors — the language does support parallelism.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A network engineer is designing a system where the data transmission requirements are as follows:
- File download: must guarantee all data is received correctly
- Live video call: prioritizes low latency; occasional missing frames are acceptable
- Online multiplayer game: real-time updates, slightly missing packets are tolerable
- Secure bank transfer: must guarantee all data and encrypt it

questions.question: Which of the following protocol assignments correctly matches each use case?

choices.text: File download: UDP; Live video call: TCP; Online game: TCP; Bank transfer: HTTPS
choices.explanation: File download needs reliable delivery (TCP, not UDP). Live video calls benefit from UDP for lower latency.

choices.text: File download: HTTPS; Live video call: HTTPS; Online game: DNS; Bank transfer: UDP
choices.explanation: DNS is not a transport protocol for game data. Bank transfers should use HTTPS, not UDP. Live video benefits from UDP, not HTTPS.

choices.text: File download: TCP; Live video call: UDP; Online game: UDP; Bank transfer: HTTPS
choices.explanation: File download needs guaranteed delivery (TCP). Live video and online games prioritize low latency over perfect delivery (UDP). Bank transfers need both reliability and encryption (HTTPS = HTTP over TLS over TCP).

choices.text: All four use cases should use TCP for maximum reliability
choices.explanation: While TCP's reliability is valuable for file downloads and bank transfers, live video and games benefit from UDP's lower latency even at the cost of occasional dropped packets.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
Three computing tasks are analyzed:

Task Alpha: Render 1,000 independent image frames for an animation
Task Beta: Run a simulation where each step depends on the result of the previous step
Task Gamma: Sort 1,000,000 numbers using a merge sort algorithm

questions.question: Which task(s) would benefit MOST from parallel computing, and which would benefit LEAST?

choices.text: All three tasks benefit equally from parallel computing
choices.explanation: Task Beta's sequential dependencies make it unable to benefit from parallelism, unlike Tasks Alpha and Gamma.

choices.text: Most: Task Beta (sequential tasks need more processing power); Least: Task Alpha (already fast)
choices.explanation: Sequential dependencies make a task UNSUITABLE for parallelism — more processors cannot help when each step must wait for the previous one.

choices.text: Most: Task Gamma (largest dataset); Least: Task Alpha (smallest dataset)
choices.explanation: Dataset size is not the key factor — independence of tasks determines parallel computing benefit. Task Alpha's complete independence makes it the best candidate.

choices.text: Most: Task Alpha (independent frames); Least: Task Beta (sequential dependencies)
choices.explanation: Task Alpha's 1,000 independent frames can each be rendered by a separate processor simultaneously — ideal for parallelism. Task Beta's sequential dependencies make parallelism impossible. Task Gamma can be partially parallelized (merge sort has parallel implementations) but has some sequential merge overhead.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student argues: 'If we use more processors, we can always make any program faster — so there is no reason to optimize code for sequential performance.'

questions.question: Which of the following best refutes the student's argument?

choices.text: Amdahl's Law shows that the sequential portion of a program creates a hard limit on speedup, regardless of the number of processors — sequential optimization can reduce this bottleneck
choices.explanation: The sequential portions cannot be parallelized. Optimizing sequential code reduces the time spent in the bottleneck, improving overall performance even in parallel programs.

choices.text: Sequential programs always run faster than parallel programs, so sequential optimization is always better
choices.explanation: Parallel computing CAN be faster for suitable tasks — this overgeneralization in the other direction is also incorrect.

choices.text: Adding more processors always costs too much money, making sequential optimization more practical
choices.explanation: While cost is a practical consideration, the student's argument is about theoretical correctness, not economics.

choices.text: Modern operating systems limit programs to using at most 4 processors, making additional optimization necessary
choices.explanation: Modern systems can use many processors — the limitation is the algorithm's sequential dependencies, not an OS limit.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A student is comparing how two countries access the global Internet:

Country X: Has five independent fiber-optic cables connecting it to the global Internet backbone.
Country Y: Has one satellite uplink providing all Internet connectivity.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly evaluates the fault tolerance and redundancy of each country's Internet connectivity?

choices.text: Country Y is more fault tolerant because satellites cover a wider geographic area
choices.explanation: Geographic coverage is not related to fault tolerance in this context. A single satellite uplink is a single point of failure — if it fails, all connectivity is lost.

choices.text: Country X is more fault tolerant because multiple connections provide redundant paths; a single cable failure does not disrupt connectivity
choices.explanation: Five independent connections provide redundancy — if one or more cables fail, the remaining connections maintain Internet access. Country Y's single uplink is a single point of failure.

choices.text: Both countries are equally fault tolerant because they are both connected to the Internet
choices.explanation: The number of independent connections determines fault tolerance. One connection (Country Y) is far less fault tolerant than five (Country X).

choices.text: Country X is less fault tolerant because having more connections creates more potential failure points
choices.explanation: More independent connections increase fault tolerance — a single failure only affects one of five paths, not all connectivity.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A student reads: 'The Internet uses open protocols documented in publicly available specifications called RFCs (Request for Comments). Any company or developer can read these specifications and build compatible systems without paying licensing fees or obtaining permission.'

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why open standards have been essential to the Internet's growth?

choices.text: Open standards force all devices to use the same hardware, ensuring compatibility
choices.explanation: Open standards specify software protocols, not hardware — they allow diverse hardware to interoperate through common communication rules.

choices.text: Open standards provide free security guarantees for all Internet communications
choices.explanation: Open standards address interoperability, not security guarantees. Security is provided by additional protocols like TLS, which are also open standards but not inherently secure by being open.

choices.text: Open standards allow any developer or manufacturer to build Internet-compatible software and hardware without proprietary restrictions, enabling innovation and global interoperability
choices.explanation: Because anyone can implement HTTP, TCP/IP, and other open protocols, billions of diverse devices from millions of manufacturers all work together — this open ecosystem drove exponential Internet growth.

choices.text: Open standards ensure that all Internet users pay the same price for connectivity
choices.explanation: Open standards address technical interoperability, not pricing of Internet access.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A scientist needs to process 1,200 independent satellite images. Each image takes 10 seconds to process on one processor. She has access to:

Option A: 6 processors
Option B: 12 processors
Option C: 100 processors

Assume no overhead or communication costs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the options from fastest to slowest completion time?

choices.text: Option C (120 sec) < Option B (1,000 sec) < Option A (2,000 sec)
choices.explanation: The calculations are incorrect. With 12 processors and 1,200 images each taking 10s: 1,200 ÷ 12 × 10 = 1,000s. With 6 processors: 1,200 ÷ 6 × 10 = 2,000s. With 100 processors: 1,200 ÷ 100 × 10 = 120s. So C < B < A in time is correct, but the values shown for B and A are wrong in the stem.

choices.text: Option A (200 sec) < Option B (100 sec) < Option C (12 sec)
choices.explanation: These values represent the number of images per processor, not the time. Each image takes 10 seconds, so multiply by 10: A=2,000s, B=1,000s, C=120s.

choices.text: All three options complete in 10 seconds because each image is processed independently
choices.explanation: Each processor handles multiple images sequentially — with 6 processors and 1,200 images, each processor handles 200 images × 10 sec = 2,000 sec total.

choices.text: Option C (120 sec) < Option B (1,000 sec) < Option A (2,000 sec) — C is fastest
choices.explanation: Option A: 1,200 images ÷ 6 processors = 200 images/processor × 10 sec = 2,000 sec. Option B: 1,200 ÷ 12 = 100 images × 10 sec = 1,000 sec. Option C: 1,200 ÷ 100 = 12 images × 10 sec = 120 sec. Ranked fastest to slowest: C (120s) < B (1,000s) < A (2,000s).
